description: Creating walls in PFC 5.0 using wall generate plane, wall generate box, and wall create vertices commands. Walls define model boundaries and loading platens in PFC simulations.

sections.heading: wall generate plane — Flat Walls
sections.content:
The simplest way to create a planar wall (infinite plane trimmed by domain extent).

Syntax:
  wall generate id N plane dip D dd DD position X Y Z

Parameters:
  id N        — wall ID number (must be unique)
  dip D       — dip angle in degrees (0-90)
  dd DD       — dip direction in degrees (0-360)
  position X Y Z — a point the plane passes through

Common orientations:
  dip 0  dd 0   — horizontal plane, normal aligned with z-axis (floor/ceiling)
  dip 90 dd 90  — vertical plane, normal aligned with x-axis (left/right walls)
  dip 90 dd 0   — vertical plane, normal aligned with y-axis (front/back walls)

Example:
  wall generate id 1 plane dip 0 dd 0 position 0 0 0.04

The plane extends across the entire domain. The domain extent (set via 'domain extent') defines the visible/active portion of the wall.

sections.heading: wall generate box — Box Walls
sections.content:
Create a closed rectangular box from 6 planar walls in one command.

Syntax:
  wall generate box x1 x2 y1 y2 z1 z2 [one-wall]

  x1, x2   — x-range of the box
  y1, y2   — y-range of the box
  z1, z2   — z-range of the box

  one-wall — optional keyword; if provided, creates a single wall (6 facets on 1 wall)
             instead of 6 separate walls. Useful for rigid box boundaries.

Without 'one-wall', 6 walls are created with IDs 1-6. The wall IDs correspond to:
  ID 1: x-min face (normal = -x)
  ID 2: x-max face (normal = +x)
  ID 3: y-min face (normal = -y)
  ID 4: y-max face (normal = +y)
  ID 5: z-min face (normal = -z)
  ID 6: z-max face (normal = +z)

Example:
  wall generate box -5.0 5.0 onewall
  wall generate box -0.025 0.025 -0.025 0.025 -0.04 0.04

sections.heading: wall create vertices — Custom Faceted Walls
sections.content:
Create custom wall shapes from triangular facets.

Syntax:
  wall create id N vertices x1 y1 z1 x2 y2 z2 x3 y3 z3 ...

  id N       — wall ID (use same ID for multiple facets belonging to the same wall)
  vertices x1 y1 z1 x2 y2 z2 x3 y3 z3 — 3D coordinates of triangle vertices

Key rules:
  - Each facet must be a triangle (exactly 3 vertices in 3D)
  - For a rectangle, you need 2 triangles (6 vertices)
  - All triangles sharing the same wall ID form a single composite wall
  - Vertices are ordered counter-clockwise to define the outward normal

Example — a rectangular wall (2 triangles):
  wall create id 1 vertices 0 0 0  1 0 0  1 1 0
  wall create id 1 vertices 0 0 0  1 1 0  0 1 0

This creates a 1x1 square in the z=0 plane using two triangles sharing the diagonal from (0,0,0) to (1,1,0).

sections.heading: Wall Attributes and Properties
sections.content:
After creation, walls can be modified with the 'wall attribute' command.

Common attributes:
  wall attribute damping D            — set wall damping coefficient
  wall attribute xvelocity V          — set x-velocity (PFC 5.0 syntax)
  wall attribute yvelocity V          — set y-velocity
  wall attribute zvelocity V          — set z-velocity
  wall attribute spin S               — set angular velocity

Wall properties for contact:
  wall property kn K                  — normal stiffness
  wall property ks K                  — shear stiffness
  wall property fric F                — friction coefficient

Note: Wall properties are set AFTER the wall is created, typically via the contact model. In PFC 5.0, walls automatically participate in contact detection; no special activation is needed.

sections.heading: Practical Notes
sections.content:
- Wall IDs must be positive integers and are assigned sequentially if omitted, but explicit IDs are recommended for clarity.
- For servo-controlled boundaries, give each wall a unique ID and reference it by ID in servo logic.
- The 'onewall' option in 'wall generate box' creates a single wall with 6 faces. This is convenient for rigid boxes but prevents individual face control (e.g., independent loading platens). For biaxial/triaxial tests, use separate walls (no onewall) so each face can move independently.
- In PFC 5.0, walls are not deformable. They act as rigid boundaries. For deformable boundaries, use a flexible membrane (ball-wall logic) or a wall composed of many small triangular facets.
- Wall stiffness should be comparable to particle stiffness for stable contact behavior. Too-stiff walls cause high contact forces; too-soft walls allow particle penetration.
